title: Understanding Your Itch — and Practical Ways to Find Relief
summary: Intense itch is one of the most challenging parts of living with psoriasis. This article explains why it happens and shares practical, evidence-informed strategies that may help.
body:
Why does psoriasis itch so much?

Itch — known medically as pruritus — is the symptom that people with psoriasis most consistently describe as their biggest daily challenge. In psoriasis, it arises from a combination of skin barrier disruption, nerve sensitisation, and inflammatory signals (particularly cytokines like IL-31) that directly activate itch-sensing nerves in the skin. It is a genuine biological process, not simply something you can will yourself to ignore.

Understanding what drives it can help you identify which factors are within your control — and that sense of agency can make a real difference day to day.

Common triggers worth knowing

Several factors are known to intensify psoriasis-related itch. Recognising your personal triggers is one of the most useful things you can do:

  • Heat and sweating — a warm environment or physical activity raises skin temperature and can rapidly increase itch signals.
  • Stress — the nervous system and immune system are closely connected. Emotional stress activates inflammatory pathways that can worsen both plaques and itch sensation.
  • Skincare products — fragrances, sodium lauryl sulphate (common in soaps and shower gels), and alcohol-based products can strip the skin barrier and intensify itch.
  • Alcohol — a known psoriasis trigger that increases skin inflammation and peripheral blood flow, both of which amplify itch.
  • Dry air — especially in winter or air-conditioned environments. Low humidity accelerates skin drying and makes plaques more prone to cracking and itching.

Breaking the scratch cycle

Scratching provides brief relief but damages the skin barrier, which drives more inflammation — and more itch. This is sometimes called the itch-scratch cycle, and interrupting it is one of the most impactful things you can work on.

A few approaches that patients find helpful:

  • Cool compresses — applying a cool, damp cloth to an itchy area temporarily damps down the nerve signals driving the itch.
  • Tapping or gentle pressure — pressing the skin near an itchy patch can interrupt the itch signal without causing damage.
  • Emollient application — keeping a fragrance-free moisturiser nearby and applying it when itch starts can both soothe and gradually strengthen the skin barrier over time.
  • Distraction — the brain can only process a limited amount of sensory input at once. Engaging activities — reading, music, a short walk — can meaningfully reduce the perceived intensity of itch.

Clothing and your environment

Clothing is often overlooked, but it makes a real difference. Loose-fitting, soft natural fibres — cotton and bamboo in particular — minimise friction and allow skin to breathe. Wool and synthetic fabrics worn directly against affected areas are common aggravators. Managing room temperature and humidity at home, especially in the bedroom, is also worth attention.

This content is for educational purposes only and is not a substitute for personalised advice from your healthcare team. If your itch is severe or significantly affecting your quality of life, please speak with your dermatologist.
